phenomenon: Quartz sand grain: erosion, transport, rounding, and deposition
key_measurement: Medium sand grain diameter 0.25–0.5 mm (Wentworth scale, 1922); sphericity increases from ~0.5 (angular) to ~0.85 (well-rounded) after >100 km fluvial transport
institutions: University of Leicester Department of Geology

sources.citation: Wentworth, C.K. (1922) 'A Scale of Grade and Class Terms for Clastic Sediments', Journal of Geology 30(5):377–392
Kuenen, Ph.H. (1960) 'Experimental abrasion 4: eolian action', Journal of Geology 68(4):427–449
USGS Mineral Resources Program — Industrial Sand and Gravel, U.S. Geological Survey Minerals Yearbook

visualization_compliance: Scale bar in millimeters required on all grain-scale frames (target ~0.25–0.5 mm for medium sand)
Show grain shape evolution sequence: angular → subangular → subrounded → rounded — label each stage
Quartz crystal structure (SiO₂, trigonal/hexagonal) must not be depicted with incorrect bond geometry
Surface texture features (conchoidal fracture, frosted pitting from aeolian abrasion) must match SEM reference imagery
Transport medium (water vs. wind) must be explicitly labeled — rounding rates differ by order of magnitude
Label ILLUSTRATIVE on cross-section diagrams; do not depict internal crystal defects without cited SEM data
